coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-decorated nano-zirconia: A highly efficient photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Ratnayake, S.P.
Mantilaka, M.M.M.G.P.G.
Sandaruwan, C.
Dahanayake, D.
Murugan, E.
Kumar, Santhosh
Amaratunga, G.A.J.
de Silva, K.M. Nalin
coredata.dc:description: A unique and efficient composite material was synthesized by incorporating carbon quantum dots on to the surface of zirconia (ZrO2) nanoparticles. ZrO2 nanoparticles with an average diameter of 40 nm were synthesized by a sol-gel process with zirconium oxychloride as the precursor. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a facile method by calcining a solution of ammonium citrate. Synthesized CQDs suspended in absolute ethanol emitted a green glow under UV irradiation. A convenient one-step process involving mixing in absolute ethanol under sonication was adopted for the formation of CQD-decorated ZrO2 nanoparticles (CQDZ). The dried final composite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The photocatalytic performance of CQDZ was evaluated by quantifying the removal efficiency of methylene blue dye. CQDZ managed to remove methylene blue by 66.5%, 76.5%, 84.3% and 95.0% under UV-irradiation of 10, 20, 40 and 60 min respectively. Under the same experimental conditions, pure ZrO2 nanoparticles managed to remove only 34% of the dye under 60 min of UV irradiation. The produced composite showed promise as a formidable contender to be used in industrial dye removal from waste water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zirconia nanoparticles
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of raman activity of CQDZ
Evaluation of bandgap and photocatalytic activity of CQDZ

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CQDs under UV irradiation following extraction in absolute ethanol.
A & B – SEM images of PZN, C & D – TEM images of PZN.
A & B – SEM images of CQDZ, C & D - TEM images of CQDZ.
EELS spectrum of CQDZ corresponding to Zr edges.
XRD patterns of PZN and CQDZ.
FT-IR spectra of solid CQDZ and PZN.
Raman spectra of: A – CQD and PZN, B – CQDZ.
A & B - Concentration change of MB dye as observed via UV spectrometry, C- First order kinetics of MB degradation by CQDZ and PZN.
A-Solid UV absorbance spectra of PZN and CQDZ, Tauc plots representing bandgap energy variations in B - PZN and C – CQDZ.
Crystallite dimensions and size of PZN and CQDZ systems.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-decorated nano-zirconia: A highly efficient photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of Colombo, Colombo 00300, Sri Lanka.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, University of Colombo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A unique and efficient composite material was synthesized by incorporating carbon quantum dots on to the surface of zirconia (ZrO2) nanoparticles. ZrO2 nanoparticles with an average diameter of 40 nm were synthesized by a sol-gel process with zirconium oxychloride as the precursor. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a facile method by calcining a solution of ammonium citrate. Synthesized CQDs suspended in absolute ethanol emitted a green glow under UV irradiation. A convenient one-step process involving mixing in absolute ethanol under sonication was adopted for the formation of CQD-decorated ZrO2 nanoparticles (CQDZ). The dried final composite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The photocatalytic performance of CQDZ was evaluated by quantifying the removal efficiency of methylene blue dye. CQDZ managed to remove methylene blue by 66.5%, 76.5%, 84.3% and 95.0% under UV-irradiation of 10, 20, 40 and 60 min respectively. Under the same experimental conditions, pure ZrO2 nanoparticles managed to remove only 34% of the dye under 60 min of UV irradiation. The produced composite showed promise as a formidable contender to be used in industrial dye removal from waste water.

ce:section.ce:para: Modern day textile industry, driven by complex consumer needs, has given rise to advanced, state of the art fabric manufacturing and processing technologies. A fabric, in its manufacturing, may undergo multiple industrial processes such as oil treatment for cohesion enhancement, enzymatic desizing, bleaching, sourcing, mercerizing, dying and printing [1,2]. Among those processes, dying is one which can give rise to a most environmentally detrimental waste effluent in the industry [3–5]. It has been estimated that over 700000 tons of synthetic dyes are annually produced and over 10000 varieties are currently in industrial use [6,7]. The current rate of human-inflicted pollution of global fresh water bodies is alarming and particularly in developing countries, textile dye is a major culprit in aquatic pollution [8,9]. Genotoxicity, mutaginity and carcinoginity are few of major harmful effects considered to be exerted by textile dyes [10]. Some dyes can be persistent and may result in bioaccumulation via food chains [11,12]. Industrial dye waste remediation is an eminent challenge at hand hence, development of efficient methods for dye removal is greatly needed.

Photocatalysis is a fascinating area having immense industrial applications, owing to which, is currently receiving high scientific interest [13–20]. Use of photocatalytic materials for water decontamination is convenient over methods such as adsorption and microbial degradation [21,22]. Nanoparticles (NPs) and nanocomposites (NCs) of a variety of metal oxides such as ZnO, TiO2, CeO2, WO3, MgO and ZrO2 have been observed to exhibit catalytic activity [23–34]. Zirconia (ZrO2) is a chemically stable, non-toxic and low-cost rare-earth oxide which is in widespread use as a modern-day high-performance engineering material [35–37]. It is known to possess a wide bandgap energy between 3.25 eV and 5.1 eV with a high negative conduction band potential [38,39]. Among the three existing phases of ZrO2, monoclinic phase has been reported to exhibit the highest catalytic activity, compared to tetragonal and cubic phases respectively [40]. Due to the wide bandgap energy of ZrO2, photoexited electrons can rapidly recombine with holes and retard the overall solar energy conversion efficiency. Approaches such as doping, incorporation of noble metals into the semiconductor matrix, organic dye-induced sensitization and coupling with semiconductors with a narrower bandgap has been attempted to address this issue [41–47]. Novel methods for improving the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 based semiconductor materials by incorporating them with only carbonaceous materials such as fullerenes, graphene, carbon quantum dots and carbon nanotubes have emerged recently [48–52]. However, to the best of our knowledge, such attempts on modification of ZrO2 by carbonaceous materials in order to synthesize efficient catalysts have not yet been reported. Nevertheless, facile methods for improving the catalytic efficiency of semiconductor materials including ZrO2 and TiO2 still need further development.

Quantum dots (QDs) belong to an outstanding category of nanomaterial due to their unique behavior and characteristics. A QD is described as an independent semiconductor nanocrystal having a size between 2–10 nm [53]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an emerging new type of QDs that are similar sized and are made up of entirely carbonaceous material [41]. CQDs have attracted significant scientific interest owing to their inert nature, low toxicity, chemical stability, high solubility and photoluminescence tunable by size [54,55]. Preparation methods for CQDs include heat induced oxidation of a molecular precursor, laser ablation or electrochemical oxidation of graphite, vapor deposition of soot, electrochemical soaking of carbon nanotubes and microwave synthesis [56–61]. CQDs are currently being used in electronic sensors and light emitting devices [62]. Further, usability of CQDs for catalytic applications in their free form or incorporated on to semiconductor NPs is being investigated. CQD composites with infra-red and visible light induced catalytic activity has been reported in literature [54,63]. The reason for enhanced photocatalytic activity has been attributed due to the conjugated structure of the CQDs, facilitating the adsorption of organic molecules on to it leading to be attacked by hydroxyl and oxygen free radicles. In such instances, a strong interaction is believed to exist between the semiconductor NP and the CQD stabilizing the composite and facilitating the charge transfer process hence leading to an enhanced photocatalytic performance [41].

In this study, we have developed a facile method to synthesize a highly efficient photocatalytic composite by adsorbing CQDs on to the surface of ZrO2 NPs. The synthesized photocatalyst is potentially suitable for convenient degradation of textile dyes. In the synthesis method of the composite (CQDZ), CQDs were synthesized by calcining an ammonium citrate solution. Prepared CQDs were then adsorbed on to the surface of ZrO2 NPs via a simple solvent based adsorption process with the assistance of ultrasound. The CQD-incorporated ZrO2 nanocomposite (CQDZ) was evaluated by means of morphology, phase composition and catalytic activity.

ZrOCl2·8H2O (98% assay, Loba chemie Inc, India) was used as the Zr source. Ammonium citrate (98% extrapure AR, Sisco research laboratories, Ltd, India) was used as the precursor in the synthesis of CQDs and ethanol absolute (VWR Chemicals, Leicestershire, UK) was used in the extraction of CQDs. Ammonia (37% assay, Sigma Aldrich, Germany) was used to adjust the pH in precipitating the Zr NPs. For all experiments, reagents and chemicals were used as received.

Four grams of ZrOCl2.8H2O was dissolved in a mixture of water and ethanol (3: 10 V/V) to prepare the zirconia sol. The mixture was kept stirring at 200 RPM for 30 min under constant sonication (Hielscher UP50H-25KHz). The pH value of the solution was monitored and kept under 1 initially. A solution of 0.1 M ammonia was added dropwise to the mixture until the formation of colloids and the pH reached ∼10. The colloidal precipitate was washed well with distilled water and calcined at 800 °C for 4 h in a muffle furnace (Nabertherm B-180) under atmospheric air. This product will be denoted as PZN hereafter.

Ammonium citrate was dissolved in distilled water to prepare a 1 M solution. Afterwards, 10 ml of the solution was placed in a Petri dish and subjected to a heat treatment at 180 °C for 3 h under atmospheric air. The resultant brown colored solid was extracted by 10 ml of absolute ethanol.

In order to adsorb CQDs on to zirconia surface, ZrO2 NPs were added into the CQD-containing ethanol solution and the mixture was sonicated for 5 min. The formed off-white suspension was dried overnight under atmospheric conditions to obtain the CQDZ.

The morphology of prepared PZN and CQDZ were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM - Hitachi SU6600 with Thermo Scientific EDX unit) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM - JEOL JEM 2100) with electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS-GATAN 963). In order to determine crystalline lattice parameters, powder X-Ray diffraction patterns were utilized. XRD patterns were obtained on Bruker d-8 Focus (40 kW, 40 mA with Cu-Kα radiation of 0.15418 nm). FT-IR spectra of ZrO2 NPs and CQDZ were obtained by Bruker Vertex 80 spectrometer using the Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR) mode. Raman measurements were conducted by Bruker Senterra II spectrometer. Bandgap energies of pure ZrO2 NPs (PZN) and CQDZ were determined using solid phase diffuse reflectance UV spectra (Shimadzu NIR-3600) with Kubelka-Munk transformations.

Methylene blue (MB) degradation efficiency was used to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of PZN and CQDZ. 0.05 g of CQDZ was dispersed in 10 ml of 10 ppm MB dye and sonicated for 30 s. Immediately after sonication, the mixture was kept under UV irradiation (170–230 nm). The procedure was repeated for CQDZ and PZN for time durations of 10, 20, 30, 40 and 60 min. Each aliquot of the mixture was centrifuged immediately at 9000 RPM after the UV irradiation and the supernatants were analysed using liquid phase UV–vis spectroscopy in the MB absorption region of 400–800 nm. The MB degradation efficiency, E was calculated by the following equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
E =
C 0
- C
C 0
× 100 %
Where C0 is the initial concentration of dye and C is the concentration of MB after UV irradiation.

After modification of ZrO2 NPs by adsorption of CQDs under sonication, a pale brown precipitate was obtained. The CQDZ was viewed under SEM which revealed no significant observable difference from previously imaged ZrO2 NPs (Fig. 3 A & B). However, when the solid CQDZ was viewed under TEM, spherical particles with a particle diameter of ∼7 nm were observed on the surface of ZrO2 NPs (Fig. 3 C & D) indicating the adsorbance of CQDs on to the surface of ZrO2 NPs.

EELS spectrum (Fig. 4 ) of CQDZ revealed K and L edges corresponding to Zr, yet no other elements were detected. This could have been due to the extremely low concentration of carbon in the suspension which was used for the TEM analysis. Direct focusing of the electron beam on to a CQD caused it to disappear either due the electron energy-induced hopping off or disintegration.

Upon analysis of solid samples by XRD (Fig. 5 ), PZN and CQDZ both were observed to be present in a multi-phase composition of monoclinic and tetragonal phases. Major peaks were detected at 2θ values: 24.32°, 28.30°, 34.52° and 50.39°. However, highest-intensity peaks were exhibited by the tetragonal phase which is in agreement with previously reported data [37]. The phase ratio calculated using peak intensities were 45% and 55% for monoclinic and tetragonal phases respectively. In CQDZ, the intensity of three crystal planes were observed to be considerably lower than those of PZN. Intensities of crystal planes belonging to (110), (020) and (120) were indicated to be reduced and this could have been caused by higher adsorption of CQDs on those specific planes.

Scherer’s formula was used to calculate crystallite sizes and dimensions of both species (Table 1 ). For PZN, average crystallite size was notably different when calculated for tetragonal and monoclinic phases separately. However, for CQDZ, crystallite sizes were comparable for both phases. Interestingly, crystallite sizes calculated for CQDZ were significantly smaller than those of PZN.

As shown in Fig. 6 , FT-IR bands of CQDZ and PZN were obtained from 500 – 4000 cm−1. Spectra of both samples showed peaks mostly corresponding to bonds present in ZrO2. Zr-O bond in monoclinic phase is represented at 585 cm−1 while CO bond is represented at 1571 cm−1. As per Bermúdez and colleagues, the peak in the vicinity of ∼1630 cm−1 and the peak within the region, 1150 – 1350 cm−1 are characteristic to tetragonal ZrO2 [64]. These peaks are low in intensity contradicting the generally known predominant tetragonal phase composition of sol-gel derived ZrO2 nanoparticles which have been reported previously [37,65]. In the case of CQDs, characteristic peaks considered to be given by CQDs were not evident except for the peak at ∼1580 cm−1 which represents skeletal vibration of sp2 and sp3 hybridized CH, CO and COH [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of raman activity of CQDZ
ce:section.ce:para: Raman spectra were obtained for PZN, CQDs and CQDZ separately (Fig. 7 ). In PZN, six prominent peaks were observed among which the strongest was at 266 cm−1. In descending intensity, peaks were present at 643, 474, 316, 145 and 181 cm−1. Multiple researchers have reported that these peaks are due to the presence of tetragonal phase of ZrO2 [27,66]. In CQDZ, strong peaks were observed at 1550 cm−1 and 1356 cm−1 which, according to Kumar and colleagues are the G-band and d-band of carbon present in the form of CQDs in the nanocomposite [67]. Spectrum intensity of CQDZ was significantly higher compared to PZN due to the higher Raman activity of carbonaceous material than inorganic ZrO2. A clear spectrum could not be obtained for CQDs suspended in ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of bandgap and photocatalytic activity of CQDZ
ce:section.ce:para:
As CQDZ and PZN were subjected to UV irradiation by exposing 0.05 g of the powder dispersed in 10 ppm of Methylene Blue (MB), a rapid removal was observed by CQDZ (Fig. 8 : A & B). The final concentration reduction of MB solution after UV exposure times of 10, 20 and 40 min are 66.5%, 76.5% and 84.3% respectively. After 60 min of exposure, 95% of MB had been degraded. At the same time, PZN with the same size and morphology, only degraded 34% of MB under the same conditions. Adsorption on to ZrO2 surface was assumed to be similar in both samples as the substrate materials in both were identical ZrO2 NPs. The photocatalytic degradation of MB by PZN and CQDZ catalysts follows first order kinetics as given in following equation.
(2)
l n
C 0
C
= k t
Where k is rate constant and t is time. Fig. 8-C shows the plot of ln(Co/C) versus the irradiation time which indicates the first order kinetics of the reaction. The rate constant (k), half-life time (t1/2 ) and linear-coefficient (R2 ) of CQDZ are 0.0533 min−1, 13.00 min and 0.9384 respectively, while the same parameters of PZN are 0.0085 min−1, 81.54 min and 0.7477 respectively. All kinetic parameters of CQDZ are lower than that of PZN (Fig. 8).

Diffuse reflectance UV spectra were obtained for both PZN and CQDZ and were subjected to Kubelka-Munk conversion to obtain absorbance spectra (Fig. 9 -A). Major peaks were observed in the range of 200–250 nm for both species. However, additional peaks were observed for CQDZ in the range of 250–400 nm.

Bandgap plots attributing to direct and indirect transitions were generated by UV data. In the case of PZN, bandgap energies corresponding to direct and indirect transitions were 4.58 and 5.12 eV respectively as opposed to corresponding values of 4.75 and 5.15 eV of CQDZ (Fig. 9-B and C).

Even though there is no notable difference between band gap energies of PZN and CQDZ, the photocatalytic activity of CQDZ is significantly greater than that of PZN. One of the major problems in many of photocatalyst materials is the recombination of electron and hole pairs which diminish the efficiency of the photocatalyst [68]. In our case, this problem has been addressed by adsorbing CQDs on the surface of photocatalysis materials. CQDs are capable of mediating the electron transfer avoiding recombination of hole and electron pairs. As a result, recombination in PZN has reduced significantly due to the surface-attached CQDs [68].

Electron Spin Resonance Spectroscopy (ESR) was conducted to investigate the behavior of unpaired electrons in the matrix and a non-first order ESR spectrum was obtained for CQDZ. It could be due to CQD holding the unpaired electron. The carbon occupying the lattice is required to have an axial symmetry (gx = gy < gz) to yield such a spectrum. As per the results, the electron seems to be fully localized on carbon (Fig. 10 ).
The paramagnetism observed can be attributed to the adsorbed CQD as it carries the unpaired electron. In addition, lattice defect sites on the surface are indicated as well.

ZrO2 is known to have a wide bandgap which has hindered its use as a direct photocatalyst as opposed to TiO2 and ZnO. However, ZrO2, possess some unique features which assist in the photocatalytic process when used in composite forms such as the one we have studied and reported here. ZrO2 is considered to have an isoelectric point of 4.1 and in the presence of pH values above this, it acts as a negatively charged surface, attracting positively charged species [69,70]. In our case, MB, the dye used was a cationic one and owing to the negative surface charge of ZrO2, MB could have adsorbed on to the surface [69,71]. On the other hand, CQDs are renowned as excellent electron reservoirs, which could actively diminish the chance of electron-hole recombination. During the UV irradiation, the MB molecules which are loosely bound to the surface stays within the vicinity of the catalytic process during which CQDs actively hinder the electron-hole recombination, resulting in a higher concentration of free radicals to degrade the dye molecules.

Facile method was developed for the synthesis of a carbon quantum dots-decorated zirconia nanoparticles by adsorbing CQDs on the surface of ZrO2 nanoparticles. The synthesized nanocomposite showed superior photocatalytic activity against Methylene Blue where 95% of the dye was degraded within 60 min of UV irradiation. On the contrary, pure ZrO2 nanoparticles showed only 34% of degradation under same conditions. CQDs seem to avoid immediate recombination of electron-hole pairs which significantly increases the photocatalytic activity of the ZrO2 composite. Therefore, the synthesized nanocomposite stands out as a formidable potential candidate in efficient removal of textile/industrial dyes from waste water.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Report on Industrial Attachment at Hamid Fabrics Limited

N. Puvaneswari, J. Muthukrishnan, P. Gunasekaran, (2006).
S. Ratnayake, M. Mantilaka, D. Dahanayake, K. De Silva, G. Amaratunga, Proceedings of the 3rd International Conference on Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Colombo, Sri Lanka 1 (2016) 85.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of methyl orange and methylene blue dyes from aqueous solution using lala clam (Orbicularia orbiculata) shell
